message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Examination of the chronology, location and size of waves of SARS-CoV-2 infection across England could shed light on the inter-play between the 1
                  <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                  and the 2
                  <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                  Waves. From mid-October onwards such an analysis becomes increasingly difficult due to the emergence of a new strain (VOC-202012/01) and in light of the differential implementation of lockdown measures and tiers. Therefore, we sought to examine trends and correlations in virus prevalence and covid-related deaths spanning the start of the UK pandemic in March 2020 through to early November 2020 – i.e., including the first growth period of the 2
                  <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                  Wave. We found striking regional relationships between the 1
                  <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                  and the 2
                  <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                  Wave that are difficult to explain other than by involving some role for changing levels of immunity in the population affecting the progression of the pandemic.
                </jats:p>
title: Non-Congruent SARS-CoV-2 Waves in England
